key_points: A balanced chemical equation must satisfy the Law of Conservation of Mass; atoms are neither created nor destroyed.
Decomposition reactions require energy in the form of heat (Thermal), light (Photolytic), or electricity (Electrolytic).
Displacement reactions depend on the reactivity series; a more reactive metal displaces a less reactive one from its salt solution.
Double displacement reactions often involve the exchange of ions between reactants to form a precipitate (Precipitation Reaction).
Redox reactions involve simultaneous oxidation (loss of electrons/gain of oxygen) and reduction (gain of electrons/loss of oxygen).
examples: Thermal Decomposition: $2Pb(NO_3)_2(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2PbO(s) + 4NO_2(g) + O_2(g)$ (Observation: Brown fumes of $NO_2$).
Photolytic Decomposition: $2AgCl(s) \xrightarrow{	ext{Sunlight}} 2Ag(s) + Cl_2(g)$ (Used in black and white photography).
Displacement: $Fe(s) + CuSO_4(aq) ightarrow FeSO_4(aq) + Cu(s)$ (Blue solution turns pale green).
Double Displacement: $Na_2SO_4(aq) + BaCl_2(aq) ightarrow BaSO_4(s) + 2NaCl(aq)$ (White precipitate formed).
Redox: $CuO + H_2 ightarrow Cu + H_2O$ ($CuO$ is reduced, $H_2$ is oxidized).
formulas: General Double Displacement: $AB + CD ightarrow AD + CB$
Corrosion of Iron (Rust): $4Fe + 3O_2 + xH_2O ightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot xH_2O$
exam_tips: Focus on the color changes in reactions, as these are frequently asked in 'Identify the substance' questions.
When identifying a Redox reaction, the 'oxidizing agent' is the substance that gets reduced, and the 'reducing agent' is the one that gets oxidized.
Ensure the number of atoms is equal on both sides; coefficients apply to the entire molecule ($2H_2O$ means 4 Hydrogen and 2 Oxygen atoms).
Remember that Rancidity can be prevented by using antioxidants or flushing containers with Nitrogen gas.
